heroOverview: Shock is a life-threatening failure of the circulatory system to deliver enough oxygen to the body's tissues, and it carries a hospital mortality rate of 20–50% depending on cause and time to treatment. The term 'unspecified shock' (ICD-10: R57.9) is applied when the underlying mechanism — whether cardiogenic, hypovolaemic, distributive, or obstructive — has not yet been established. Every minute of inadequately treated shock worsens cellular injury, because organs deprived of oxygen switch to anaerobic metabolism, producing lactic acid. The most important fact about shock is that it is a medical emergency requiring immediate recognition and treatment; outcome is tightly linked to how quickly the cause is found and reversed.

keyStats.prevalence: Shock complicates approximately 1–2% of all emergency department presentations in high-income countries; in-hospital mortality ranges from 20% for hypovolaemic shock to above 50% for cardiogenic shock (ESC/ACC guidelines)
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; septic shock peaks in adults over 65, cardiogenic shock in middle-aged to elderly adults with cardiac disease, hypovolaemic shock in young adults from trauma
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak — depends heavily on underlying cause
keyStats.globalCases: Septic shock alone causes an estimated 265,000 deaths per year in the United States (SCCM, 2022)

definition: Shock (ICD-10: R57.9) is a state of acute circulatory failure in which tissue perfusion is inadequate to meet cellular metabolic demands. At the cellular level, insufficient oxygen delivery triggers anaerobic glycolysis, lactate accumulation, ATP depletion, and eventual cell death. The clinical hallmarks are hypotension (systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg or a drop of more than 40 mmHg from baseline), end-organ dysfunction (altered mental status, oliguria, cold or mottled extremities), and a raised serum lactate above 2 mmol/L. The 'unspecified' classification is used when a type has not yet been determined and serves as the working diagnosis during initial resuscitation. The four physiological categories that all forms of shock fall into are: hypovolaemic (inadequate intravascular volume), cardiogenic (pump failure), distributive (maldistribution of blood flow, as in sepsis or anaphylaxis), and obstructive (mechanical obstruction to flow, as in tension pneumothorax or massive pulmonary embolism). Each category demands a different definitive treatment, which is why rapid identification of the cause is part of the resuscitation process.

typesClassification.description: Caused by loss of circulating volume through haemorrhage, severe dehydration, burns, or third-spacing; the heart receives inadequate preload and cardiac output falls.
The heart fails as a pump — most commonly after a large myocardial infarction — so adequate output cannot be maintained despite normal or high filling pressures.

Pathological vasodilation or maldistribution of blood flow; vessels dilate so widely that the same cardiac output fails to perfuse tissues adequately. Septic shock is the most common subtype worldwide.
A mechanical barrier — tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, or massive pulmonary embolism — prevents adequate filling or ejection of the heart despite normal volume and pump function.
The working diagnosis when the mechanism is not yet determined; immediate resuscitation begins while investigations identify the cause.

primarySymptoms: Hypotension — systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg or a fall of more than 40 mmHg from the patient's usual baseline, the most widely used haemodynamic marker.
Tachycardia — resting heart rate above 100 beats per minute, the earliest compensatory response to falling perfusion pressure.
Altered mental status — ranging from agitation and confusion to drowsiness and unresponsiveness, reflecting reduced cerebral perfusion.
Cold, clammy, and mottled skin — caused by peripheral vasoconstriction redirecting blood to vital organs; absent in distributive shock, where skin is often warm.
Oliguria or anuria — urine output falling below 0.5 mL/kg/hour, an early marker of renal hypoperfusion.
Rapid, shallow breathing (tachypnoea) — a compensatory response to metabolic acidosis from lactate accumulation.
Prolonged capillary refill time — more than 2 seconds after blanching the nail bed, reflecting impaired peripheral circulation.
Weak or thready peripheral pulses — reduced pulse pressure signals falling stroke volume.
Dizziness or syncope on standing — postural hypotension from volume depletion or vasodilation.
Nausea and vomiting — from splanchnic hypoperfusion and autonomic activation.
earlyWarningSigns: Resting heart rate consistently above 90 beats per minute without an obvious cause.
Blood pressure that feels 'borderline low' for the patient or drops on standing.
Increasing thirst, reduced urine output, or dark urine over several hours.
Unusual pallor, sweating, and weakness together after an injury, procedure, or acute illness.
An early warning score (NEWS2 ≥ 5) or rapid-response trigger on a hospital ward.
emergencySigns: Systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg — call emergency services immediately.
Unresponsiveness, obtundation, or seizure in the context of preceding illness or injury.
Severe difficulty breathing alongside hypotension or absent pulse — suggests obstructive or cardiogenic shock.
Absent radial pulse with rapid, gasping breathing — pre-arrest state requiring immediate resuscitation.
Signs of severe allergic reaction (urticaria, stridor, angioedema) combined with hypotension — anaphylactic shock.
High fever with confusion and hypotension — hallmarks of septic shock requiring antibiotics within one hour.

causes.description: Loss of more than 30% of circulating blood volume — from trauma, gastrointestinal bleeding, or surgical bleeding — drops venous return so steeply that cardiac output cannot be sustained despite compensatory tachycardia.

Destruction of more than 40% of the left ventricular myocardium by infarction impairs contractility so severely that the heart cannot maintain forward flow; cardiogenic shock complicates 5–10% of ST-elevation MIs.

Bacterial, fungal, or viral infection triggers a dysregulated immune response releasing cytokines that cause widespread endothelial injury, capillary leak, and vasodilation, reducing effective circulating volume and systemic vascular resistance simultaneously.

IgE-mediated or direct mast-cell degranulation causes sudden massive histamine release, producing profound vasodilation and capillary permeability within minutes of allergen exposure, collapsing arterial pressure.

Air accumulating under pressure in the pleural space compresses the mediastinum, kinks the superior vena cava, and prevents ventricular filling — the classic cause of obstructive shock from chest injury or mechanical ventilation.

Profuse vomiting, diarrhoea, burns over more than 20% body surface area, or high-output fistulae can reduce intravascular volume by enough to precipitate hypovolaemic shock, particularly in the elderly and in children.

A massive embolus blocking the main pulmonary arteries raises right ventricular afterload acutely; the right ventricle dilates and fails, septum shifts left, and left ventricular filling collapses, producing obstructive shock.

riskFactors.description: Older adults have reduced cardiac reserve, blunted autonomic responses, and impaired renal autoregulation, making compensation less effective and the transition to overt shock faster.
Prior myocardial infarction, heart failure, or severe aortic stenosis reduces the cardiac reserve available to compensate for any additional haemodynamic stress.
Corticosteroid use, chemotherapy, HIV, or asplenia impairs the ability to contain infection, raising the risk that sepsis progresses to septic shock before it is recognised.
Warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, or inherited coagulopathies increase the haemorrhagic response to any injury or procedure, accelerating progression to hypovolaemic shock.
Autonomic neuropathy blunts the tachycardic and vasoconstrictive responses to hypotension; diabetic vasculopathy impairs organ perfusion at higher blood pressure thresholds.
A previous anaphylactic reaction is the strongest predictor of future anaphylaxis; not carrying or using prescribed adrenaline auto-injectors is the main modifiable risk.
Central venous lines, urinary catheters, and surgical procedures are the leading sources of bacteraemia that can progress to septic shock, particularly in the critically ill.
Haemodynamic changes of pregnancy (expanded volume but low systemic vascular resistance) alter shock thresholds; obstetric haemorrhage and puerperal sepsis are leading maternal causes of shock.

affectedDemographics: Trauma patients of any age, especially young males in whom haemorrhage is the leading cause
Elderly adults with cardiac disease, who are at highest risk of cardiogenic shock
Adults over 65 with chronic illness, the primary septic shock population
Children with severe gastroenteritis or sepsis, in whom dehydration and infection interact rapidly
Postoperative patients and those with indwelling lines or catheters
Individuals with known severe allergies who have had anaphylaxis before
diagnosisOverview: Shock is a clinical diagnosis: the triad of hypotension, tachycardia, and signs of end-organ hypoperfusion establishes it in minutes at the bedside. The parallel task — identifying the type — requires a focused history (injury, infection, allergen exposure, cardiac history), physical examination (warm versus cold extremities, jugular venous pressure, breath sounds, skin findings), and a rapid set of investigations. Bedside point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) has become central: cardiac windows assess ventricular function and pericardial effusion; IVC collapsibility estimates volume status; lung windows exclude pneumothorax and pulmonary oedema. Blood tests include full blood count (anaemia from haemorrhage, leucocytosis from sepsis), serum lactate (elevated above 2 mmol/L confirms inadequate perfusion regardless of blood pressure), arterial blood gas (metabolic acidosis), renal function, troponin (myocardial injury), coagulation screen, blood cultures, and cross-match. An ECG is obtained immediately to exclude infarction. Chest X-ray is performed but should not delay resuscitation. CT angiography is definitive for pulmonary embolism and aortic dissection when the patient is stable enough to transfer. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign and ATLS guidelines provide cause-specific diagnostic algorithms that run in parallel with resuscitation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the severity of tissue hypoperfusion regardless of blood pressure; initial lactate above 4 mmol/L predicts high short-term mortality and triggers aggressive resuscitation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample drawn from an arterial line or peripheral vein; result available in 5–10 minutes on a blood gas analyser.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapidly classifies the type of shock by assessing cardiac contractility, pericardial effusion, IVC filling, and lung aeration at the bedside.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An emergency physician or intensivist applies a probe to the chest and abdomen; takes 3–5 minutes and requires no contrast or radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects metabolic acidosis (low pH, low bicarbonate, high base deficit) and respiratory compensation, confirming inadequate perfusion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small blood draw from the radial or femoral artery; results returned in minutes from a bedside analyser.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, metabolic panel, and coagulation screen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies haemorrhage (low haemoglobin), infection (elevated white count), renal failure (raised creatinine), and disseminated intravascular coagulation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venepuncture; results in 30–60 minutes from the hospital laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes ST-elevation myocardial infarction or arrhythmia as the cause of cardiogenic shock; troponin confirms myocardial injury.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: ECG is non-invasive and takes about 5 minutes; troponin result from a blood draw is available in 30–60 minutes with high-sensitivity assays.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms or excludes massive pulmonary embolism as the cause of obstructive shock in haemodynamically borderline patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A CT scanner with intravenous contrast; takes about 15 minutes but requires transferring the patient from the resuscitation area.

treatmentOverview: The foundational steps for unspecified shock are the same regardless of cause: protect the airway, ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation, and restore perfusion pressure with intravenous fluid and vasopressors while simultaneously identifying the underlying mechanism. High-flow supplemental oxygen is given immediately. Two large-bore peripheral IV lines are inserted and a rapid intravenous fluid challenge — typically 500 mL of isotonic crystalloid over 15 minutes — is given to assess volume responsiveness; patients whose blood pressure and cardiac output improve with fluid are volume-responsive. Persistent hypotension after fluid resuscitation requires vasopressors: noradrenaline is first-line for distributive shock (septic, neurogenic), vasopressin can be added as a second agent, and adrenaline is the drug of choice for anaphylaxis. Cardiogenic shock requires inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone) rather than fluid loading, and may need urgent coronary revascularisation, mechanical circulatory support (intra-aortic balloon pump, Impella), or arrhythmia treatment. Haemorrhagic shock responds to damage-control haemostasis, tranexamic acid, and massive transfusion protocols with balanced blood products. Septic shock demands IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour, source control, and the Surviving Sepsis Campaign bundle. Obstructive shock requires immediate relief of the obstruction — needle decompression for tension pneumothorax, pericardiocentesis for tamponade, systemic thrombolysis or catheter-directed treatment for massive pulmonary embolism. Critical care monitoring in an ICU environment is the standard of care once the patient is stabilised.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous fluid resuscitation
medicalTreatments.description: Isotonic crystalloid (0.9% saline or Hartmann's solution) is the first-line resuscitation fluid; 500 mL boluses are given and the response in blood pressure and urine output assessed. Over-resuscitation is harmful — fluid is withheld once the patient is volume-unresponsive.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores perfusion pressure in approximately 50% of hypotensive patients when the cause is hypovolaemia or early distributive shock; ineffective and harmful in cardiogenic shock.

medicalTreatments.name: Noradrenaline (norepinephrine)
medicalTreatments.description: The first-line vasopressor for septic and other distributive shock; raises systemic vascular resistance to restore a mean arterial pressure of at least 65 mmHg. Administered via central venous catheter as a continuous infusion, titrated to effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves a MAP above 65 mmHg in over 90% of septic shock patients as a single agent; mortality benefit over dopamine demonstrated in randomised trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Adrenaline (epinephrine)
medicalTreatments.description: Intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg is the first-line and life-saving treatment for anaphylactic shock; it reverses vasodilation, reduces capillary permeability, and bronchodilates simultaneously. IV infusion is used for refractory shock.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces anaphylaxis mortality by an estimated 80–90% when given within minutes; delay beyond 30 minutes is associated with markedly higher fatality rates.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics
medicalTreatments.description: For suspected septic shock, empirical antibiotics covering Gram-negative and Gram-positive organisms are given within one hour of recognition; specific regimens follow local protocols and are narrowed once cultures return.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Each hour of delay in antibiotic administration for septic shock is associated with a 7–10% increase in mortality in landmark observational studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Blood product transfusion and tranexamic acid
medicalTreatments.description: Massive transfusion protocols using packed red cells, fresh frozen plasma, and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio restore oxygen-carrying capacity and coagulation simultaneously; tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours reduces haemorrhagic mortality by approximately 15% (CRASH-2 trial).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Balanced transfusion reduces 28-day mortality compared to crystalloid-dominant resuscitation; tranexamic acid reduced all-cause mortality by 9% in the landmark CRASH-2 RCT.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrocortisone (IV corticosteroids)
medicalTreatments.description: IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day is recommended for septic shock that remains vasopressor-dependent after adequate fluid and vasopressor therapy; it accelerates haemodynamic stabilisation and vasopressor weaning.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces time on vasopressors and may reduce 90-day mortality by approximately 3–5% in vasopressor-dependent septic shock (ADRENAL trial).

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency haemorrhage control surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Damage-control surgery to pack haemorrhage, ligate bleeding vessels, or repair injured organs in haemorrhagic shock; the minimum intervention to achieve haemostasis, with definitive repair deferred until physiology is corrected.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Survival to hospital discharge in about 60–70% of severely injured patients when combined with damage-control resuscitation.

surgicalOptions.name: Needle thoracostomy and chest drain
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency decompression of tension pneumothorax — a 14-gauge needle inserted in the second intercostal space, followed by a formal chest drain — is immediately life-saving in obstructive shock from pneumothorax.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Nearly 100% effective when performed promptly in confirmed tension pneumothorax.

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency coronary revascularisation (PCI)
surgicalOptions.description: Percutaneous coronary intervention to restore coronary flow in cardiogenic shock complicating ST-elevation myocardial infarction; combined with mechanical circulatory support if needed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces 1-year mortality from approximately 60% (without reperfusion) to 40–50% in cardiogenic shock (SHOCK trial data).

alternativeTreatments.name: Positioning (Trendelenburg or legs elevated)
alternativeTreatments.description: Raising the legs 15–30° transiently increases venous return and is used as an immediate, no-cost intervention to temporarily improve blood pressure in hypovolaemic and distributive shock while IV access is secured.

alternativeTreatments.name: MAST (military anti-shock trousers) — historical
alternativeTreatments.description: External pneumatic compression garments can transiently redistribute blood volume; now largely replaced by damage-control resuscitation, but still occasionally used in resource-limited pre-hospital settings for pelvic fractures.

whySeeSpecialist: Shock is managed by emergency physicians and intensivists, with specialist input depending on cause: cardiologists for cardiogenic shock, surgeons and haematologists for haemorrhagic shock, clinical microbiologists and infectious disease specialists for septic shock. Transfer to a cardiac arrest centre or major trauma centre, where all specialties are immediately available, significantly improves outcomes. No part of shock management should be handled in a non-monitored setting.
doctorSelectionGuide: For a patient presenting with undifferentiated shock, the priority is a hospital emergency department with a resuscitation bay, 24-hour critical care, and rapid access to echocardiography and interventional radiology or cardiology. For long-term follow-up after a shock episode — post-ICU syndrome, recurrent anaphylaxis, or cardiogenic shock — an intensivist-led post-ICU clinic, cardiologist, or allergist-immunologist respectively is appropriate.
preventionStrategies: Carry and know how to use an adrenaline auto-injector (EpiPen) if you have a history of anaphylaxis.
Adhere to anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy if prescribed for cardiac or vascular disease to reduce infarct size.
Seek prompt medical care at the earliest signs of serious infection — fever with confusion, hypotension, or rigors — to prevent progression to septic shock.
Follow surgical site care instructions and report early wound infection signs after operations.
Ensure vaccinations against pneumococcus, influenza, and meningococcus are up to date, particularly for asplenic individuals.
Wear seatbelts and use appropriate protective equipment for activities associated with trauma risk.
lifestyleModifications: People with known severe allergies should wear medical alert identification and avoid documented allergens consistently.
Patients with heart failure should weigh themselves daily and report rapid weight gain (more than 2 kg in 48 hours) promptly.
Stay well hydrated in hot weather and during gastrointestinal illness, especially in elderly individuals and children.
Take prescribed medications for chronic cardiac, endocrine, or infectious conditions reliably — abrupt discontinuation can precipitate haemodynamic crises.
Report significant bleeding — black or tarry stools, coughing blood, or blood in the urine — to a clinician without delay.

recommended: Adequate salt and fluid intake in individuals with orthostatic hypotension or vasovagal syncope to maintain intravascular volume
A heart-healthy diet low in saturated fat and high in vegetables and whole grains for those with cardiovascular risk factors
Consistent meal timing for patients on insulin to avoid hypoglycaemia-triggered autonomic responses
avoid: Known allergens without exception in individuals with a history of anaphylaxis
Excessive alcohol, which causes vasodilation and can precipitate hypotension in volume-depleted individuals
High-sodium processed foods in heart failure patients, which worsen fluid retention and increase haemodynamic stress

exerciseGuidelines: During and immediately after a shock episode, all physical activity is contraindicated. Following full recovery, a supervised cardiac rehabilitation programme is appropriate after cardiogenic shock. Patients who have survived septic shock benefit from graduated physical rehabilitation to address ICU-acquired weakness and post-intensive care syndrome. Return to full activity should be guided by an intensivist or rehabilitation physician with reassessment of functional capacity.
dailyManagement: Post-shock patients should attend all scheduled outpatient follow-up appointments — post-ICU clinics address psychological, cognitive, and physical recovery.
Wear medical-alert jewellery for known anaphylaxis triggers, cardiac implants, or anticoagulation status.
Maintain a written list of triggers and prior shock episodes for any emergency clinician to review.
Monitor blood pressure at home if prescribed anti-hypertensives after cardiogenic shock and report readings below 90/60 mmHg.
Recognise early warning signs of recurrence and act without delay — do not 'wait and see' with falling blood pressure or racing pulse.
prognosis: Outcome from shock is directly related to the speed of recognition, the speed of cause-specific reversal, and baseline comorbidities. Hypovolaemic shock from trauma in an otherwise healthy adult, treated within the 'golden hour', carries survival rates above 80%. Cardiogenic shock complicating myocardial infarction has a 30-day mortality of approximately 40–50% even with revascularisation. Septic shock mortality in high-income ICUs averages 25–35%, though it reaches above 50% in the elderly with multiorgan failure. Obstructive shock from tension pneumothorax has near-100% survival when decompressed promptly and near-100% mortality if missed. Survivors of prolonged shock frequently experience post-intensive care syndrome — a constellation of cognitive impairment, psychological disorders (anxiety, depression, PTSD), and neuromuscular weakness — that can persist for years and requires structured rehabilitation.
recoveryTimeline: The acute resuscitation phase lasts hours to days. Once perfusion is restored and the cause reversed, most patients require 3–10 days of monitored ICU care for organ support weaning and infection control. For septic or cardiogenic shock requiring ventilation, ICU stays of 1–3 weeks are common. Physical rehabilitation to regain strength lost through critical illness can take 3–6 months. Cognitive and psychological recovery may take 12 months or longer, and some impairment is permanent. Return to work and full independent function is achieved by approximately 50% of survivors at one year.
complications: Acute kidney injury — the kidneys are exquisitely sensitive to hypoperfusion; approximately 40–50% of septic shock patients develop AKI requiring at least transient renal replacement therapy.
Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) — cytokine-driven lung injury from septic or haemorrhagic shock causes bilateral pulmonary infiltrates and refractory hypoxaemia.
Myocardial injury — 'shock cardiomyopathy' can develop even in non-cardiogenic forms of shock, impairing recovery.
Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) — consumptive coagulopathy triggered by systemic inflammation leads to simultaneous thrombosis and haemorrhage.
Post-intensive care syndrome (PICS) — cognitive impairment, anxiety, depression, PTSD, and profound muscle weakness that persist after ICU discharge.
Death — despite modern intensive care, in-hospital mortality from shock remains 20–50% depending on cause and comorbidities.

supportResources.name: Surviving Sepsis Campaign — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Patient and family resources from the Society of Critical Care Medicine on understanding and recovering from septic shock.

supportResources.name: PICS Foundation — Post-Intensive Care Syndrome
supportResources.description: Resources for ICU survivors and families navigating the long-term physical, cognitive, and psychological effects after critical illness.

supportResources.name: Anaphylaxis Campaign (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing information on anaphylaxis prevention, management plans, and living with severe allergy.

sources.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021
Cecconi M et al. Consensus on circulatory shock and haemodynamic monitoring. Task force of the European Society of Intensive Care Medicine. Intensive Care Medicine, 2014
CRASH-2 Trial — Effect of tranexamic acid on death, vascular occlusive events, and blood transfusion. Lancet, 2010
ESC Guidelines on acute myocardial infarction in patients presenting with ST-segment elevation 2017
ADRENAL Trial — Adjunctive glucocorticoid therapy in patients with septic shock. NEJM, 2018
NICE Guideline NG51 — Sepsis: recognition, diagnosis and early management

confusedWithConditions.name: Emotional shock or stress reaction
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Emotional shock is a psychological response to a traumatic event with normal vital signs; circulatory shock involves haemodynamic collapse with measurable hypotension, tachycardia, and end-organ dysfunction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Severe sepsis without septic shock
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Severe sepsis causes organ dysfunction from infection but blood pressure can be maintained without vasopressors; septic shock requires vasopressors to achieve MAP ≥65 mmHg despite adequate fluid.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart failure (acute decompensated)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute decompensated heart failure produces pulmonary oedema and breathlessness with preserved or elevated blood pressure; cardiogenic shock adds refractory hypotension and peripheral hypoperfusion requiring vasopressors.

relatedConditions.name: Cardiogenic shock
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common identified sub-type of shock, arising from cardiac pump failure, and the form with the highest mortality despite revascularisation.

relatedConditions.name: Hypovolaemic shock
relatedConditions.relevance: Loss of circulating volume — from haemorrhage, dehydration, or burns — is the most common cause of shock in trauma.

relatedConditions.name: Septic shock
relatedConditions.relevance: The most prevalent form of distributive shock worldwide, occurring when systemic infection triggers irreversible vasodilation and myocardial depression.

relatedConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
relatedConditions.relevance: Anaphylaxis is the most time-critical cause of distributive shock, requiring adrenaline within minutes to prevent death.

faqs.question: What is shock in medical terms?
faqs.answer: In medicine, shock means the circulatory system is failing to deliver enough oxygen to the body's organs. It causes low blood pressure, a fast heart rate, and signs of organ damage such as confusion and reduced urine output. It is a medical emergency.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of shock?
faqs.answer: The earliest signs of shock are a fast heart rate, low or falling blood pressure, pale or clammy skin, and feeling dizzy or faint. Confusion, reduced urine, and rapid breathing follow. Any of these after injury or serious illness needs immediate emergency care.

faqs.question: How is shock treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. All shock patients need oxygen, intravenous fluids, and monitoring. Septic shock requires antibiotics urgently. Anaphylactic shock needs intramuscular adrenaline immediately. Cardiogenic shock may need drugs to support the heart or emergency heart procedures. Treatment always takes place in a hospital.

faqs.question: What are the four types of shock?
faqs.answer: The four types are hypovolaemic (low blood volume from bleeding or dehydration), cardiogenic (heart pump failure), distributive (blood vessels dilate abnormally, as in sepsis or anaphylaxis), and obstructive (a blockage prevents the heart from pumping, such as a tension pneumothorax or large blood clot in the lungs).

faqs.question: Is shock always life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Shock is always a medical emergency because even mild cases can worsen rapidly. Without treatment, all types of shock cause progressive organ failure and death. Outcomes depend heavily on how quickly treatment begins — early intervention in the first hour dramatically improves survival.

faqs.question: What is septic shock?
faqs.answer: Septic shock is a severe form of shock caused by a life-threatening infection. The immune response to infection causes blood vessels to dilate so widely that blood pressure collapses and organs begin to fail. It requires urgent antibiotics, intravenous fluids, and vasopressors in an ICU.

faqs.question: What is cardiogenic shock?
faqs.answer: Cardiogenic shock happens when the heart cannot pump enough blood to meet the body's needs, most often after a large heart attack. Blood pressure falls, skin becomes cold and clammy, and organs begin to fail. Emergency treatment may include heart procedures to restore blood flow and medications to support the heart.

faqs.question: What does 'unspecified shock' mean on a diagnosis?
faqs.answer: Unspecified shock means the doctor has confirmed circulatory collapse but has not yet identified the specific cause (such as bleeding, heart failure, or infection). It is a working code used during initial emergency treatment while investigations are underway to find and treat the underlying problem.

faqs.question: How is lactate related to shock?
faqs.answer: When organs don't receive enough oxygen in shock, they produce lactic acid. A blood lactate level above 2 mmol/L confirms inadequate perfusion even if blood pressure is still borderline. A level above 4 mmol/L signals severe shock and triggers aggressive resuscitation protocols in most guidelines.

faqs.question: Can shock happen without low blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Yes. In compensated shock, the body raises the heart rate and constricts blood vessels to maintain blood pressure temporarily. During this phase, blood pressure may appear normal but tissue perfusion is already inadequate. Raised heart rate, cold extremities, and elevated lactate can reveal shock before blood pressure falls.

faqs.question: What is the difference between shock and fainting?
faqs.answer: Fainting (syncope) is a brief loss of consciousness from a temporary drop in brain blood flow that resolves quickly and completely on lying down. Shock is a sustained failure of organ perfusion that does not resolve with lying down and causes progressive deterioration without medical treatment. Someone who does not regain consciousness quickly after fainting needs emergency assessment.

faqs.question: What fluids are given in shock?
faqs.answer: Isotonic crystalloids — normal saline or Hartmann's solution — are given first in 500 mL boluses to assess response. In haemorrhagic shock, blood products (red cells, plasma, and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio) are preferred over large volumes of saline. In cardiogenic shock, fluid is given cautiously and vasopressors are often needed instead.

faqs.question: How long does shock last?
faqs.answer: The acute haemodynamic phase of shock lasts hours to days with intensive treatment. After stabilisation, ICU admission for organ support typically lasts 3–10 days. Full physical recovery may take weeks to months, and cognitive or psychological effects (post-intensive care syndrome) can persist for a year or longer in severe cases.

faqs.question: What happens to the body during shock?
faqs.answer: As oxygen delivery falls, cells switch from normal aerobic to anaerobic metabolism, generating lactic acid. Without oxygen, ATP production fails, cell membranes break down, and organs stop functioning. The kidneys stop producing urine, the brain becomes confused, the gut loses its barrier function, and the heart weakens — a cascade that becomes irreversible without rapid treatment.

faqs.question: Can shock cause permanent damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prolonged shock can cause permanent kidney damage requiring dialysis, lasting cognitive impairment, heart muscle scarring, and significant neuromuscular weakness. Post-intensive care syndrome affects cognitive, psychological, and physical function in up to 50% of survivors, sometimes for years. Early, aggressive treatment limits the duration of organ hypoperfusion and reduces the risk of permanent harm.

faqs.question: What is the role of vasopressors in shock?
faqs.answer: Vasopressors are drugs that constrict blood vessels to raise blood pressure when fluids alone are insufficient. Noradrenaline is the first-line agent for septic shock. Adrenaline is used in anaphylaxis and as a second agent in refractory shock. Vasopressors are titrated to maintain a mean arterial pressure of at least 65 mmHg, the threshold below which kidney and brain perfusion becomes unreliable.

faqs.question: Can shock be prevented?
faqs.answer: Many causes of shock are preventable. Carrying an adrenaline auto-injector prevents death from anaphylaxis. Treating infection early prevents septic shock. Optimal management of heart disease reduces cardiogenic shock risk. Wearing seatbelts and protective equipment reduces haemorrhagic shock from trauma. Vaccination reduces the incidence of infections that can become septic.

faqs.question: What is post-intensive care syndrome after shock?
faqs.answer: Post-intensive care syndrome (PICS) is a constellation of new or worsened cognitive, psychiatric, and physical problems that persist after discharge from an ICU. Up to 50% of shock survivors experience impaired memory and concentration, anxiety, depression, or PTSD, and significant muscle weakness. Structured post-ICU follow-up clinics and rehabilitation programmes improve recovery.

faqs.question: Why does shock cause confusion?
faqs.answer: The brain requires a continuous supply of oxygen-rich blood and is highly sensitive to reductions in perfusion. In shock, falling blood pressure reduces cerebral blood flow below the threshold for normal neuronal function, producing confusion, agitation, drowsiness, and, in severe cases, unresponsiveness. Confusion improving with resuscitation is a sign of restored cerebral perfusion.

faqs.question: When should I call emergency services for shock?
faqs.answer: Call emergency services immediately if someone has low blood pressure with any of: confusion or loss of consciousness, rapid or shallow breathing, cold clammy skin, a fast thready pulse, severe allergic reaction, heavy uncontrolled bleeding, or high fever with rigors. Do not wait for all signs to be present — suspected shock is an emergency.

metaDescription: Shock is a life-threatening drop in blood flow to organs, causing low BP, confusion and organ failure. Learn types, emergency treatment and survival. Doctor-reviewed.
